name: Bannanje Govindacharya
dates_notes: Born 3 August 1936 in Bannañje, Udupi district, Karnataka; died 13 December 2020. Tulu-speaking Śivaḷḷi Brahmin Mādhva family. Padma Śrī 2009; Sāhitya Akademi awards.

dates_evidence.description: Government of India Padma Śrī award, 2009; Saṃskṛta-Sāhitya Akademi awards.
Pūrṇaprajña Vidyāpīṭha publication colophons; Sāgar Publications colophons.

core_thesis: Bannañje Govindācārya is the modern Mādhva scholar whose Sanskrit philological-philosophical output — approximately 4,000 pages of Sanskrit *vyākhyāna* across some 150 books — re-establishes Madhva's full *Sarva-Mūla-Granthāḥ* as a critically-edited, textually-stable corpus (Bannañje, ed., *Sarva-Mūla-Granthāḥ*, Pūrṇaprajña Vidyāpīṭha edition, 38 vols., general Introduction to vol. 1) and supplies Dvaita commentaries on the *Veda-Sūktas*, the principal Upaniṣads, the *Śata-Rudriya*, the *Brahma-Sūtra*, the *Bhagavad-Gītā*, the *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*, and the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* (cf. the *Sarva-Mūla* edition vols. on *Ṛg-Bhāṣya*, *Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas*, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, *Gītā-Bhāṣya*, *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*, and *Bhāgavata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*). Trained at Pūrṇaprajña Vidyāpīṭha, Bengaluru, he combined Mahāmahopādhyāya-grade traditional learning with modern critical-editorial method. Three distinctive emphases run through the work. First, the textual recovery of Madhva's intended readings against later scholastic accretion (Bannañje, *Sarva-Mūla* edition, Introductions to the *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* and *Anuvyākhyāna* volumes). Second, the defense of the historical and philosophical priority of the *Bhāgavata*-tradition's Mādhva interpretation (Bannañje, *Bhāgavata-Vyākhyāna*, Introduction to vol. 1; cf. *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya Sanskrit Vyākhyāna*, prefatory remarks). Third, work conducted simultaneously within the Udupi *aṣṭa-maṭha* sectarian apparatus and within academic Indology (Bannañje, *Ācārya Madhva: Baḍuku-Bareha*, Kannada, Sāgar Publications edition, 'Pīṭhike' / Introduction). He is the principal modern conduit through whom Madhva's full corpus reaches contemporary readers, and the Dvaita school's premier twentieth- and early-twenty-first-century philological figure.

engaged_works.title: Critical edition of Sarva-Mula-Granthah
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored editorial labour; the modern textus receptus for Madhva's full surviving Sanskrit corpus.
engaged_works.summary: A multi-volume critical edition of Madhva's surviving Sanskrit corpus — the *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, the *Anuvyākhyāna*, the *Gītā-Bhāṣya*, the Daśa-Prakaraṇa polemical treatises (*Tattvodyota*, *Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana*, *Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana*, *Upādhi-Khaṇḍana*, *Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya*, *Kathā-Lakṣaṇa*, *Pramāṇa-Lakṣaṇa*, *Karma-Nirṇaya*, *Tattva-Saṅkhyāna*, *Tattva-Viveka*), the Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas, the *Ṛg-Bhāṣya*, the *Bhāgavata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*, and the *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* (Bannañje, ed., *Sarva-Mūla-Granthāḥ*, Pūrṇaprajña Vidyāpīṭha, vols. 1–38, table of contents). The editorial labour is the principal documentary infrastructure on which contemporary Dvaita scholarship depends. Each text is presented with manuscript collation, the textus receptus, and editorial notes; the introductions to several volumes function as short Mādhva *prakaraṇas* in their own right (cf. introductions to vols. 1 *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, vol. 4 *Daśa-Prakaraṇa*, and vol. 17 *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*). The edition is the standard modern reference for the Mādhva corpus and supersedes earlier partial editions (e.g., the Belur Maṭha 1969 partial edition) for textual stability.

engaged_works.title: Sanskrit Vyakhyana on Mahabharata-Tatparya-Nirnaya
engaged_works.summary: A multi-volume Sanskrit *vyākhyāna* on Madhva's *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* (Bannañje, *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya Sanskrit Vyākhyāna*, Pūrṇaprajña Vidyāpīṭha edition, vols. 1–6). The *Tātparya-Nirṇaya* is Madhva's verse-summary of the *Mahābhārata*'s doctrinal *tātparya*, on which the school's narrative theology rests (cf. *MBTN* 1.1–10, *prathama-adhyāya* opening); Bannañje's commentary is the principal modern reading, supplying both the philological apparatus and the Mādhva doctrinal disambiguation at each contested locus. The treatment of the Bhīṣma-parvan *Gītā* segments (*MBTN* adhy. 27, on Gītā 2–18) and of the *Sahasranāma*-segment (*MBTN* adhy. 28–30, on Anuśāsana-parvan 149) is unusually detailed, and brings the *Tātparya-Nirṇaya* into explicit dialogue with the *Sarva-Mūla* polemical treatises (Bannañje *Vyākhyāna* on *MBTN* adhy. 27, cross-references to *Tattvodyota* and *Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana*). The work is the natural complement to the *Bhāgavata*-vyākhyāna on the Vaiṣṇava-narrative side, and supplies the modern Dvaita reading of the *Mahābhārata* that grounds the school's narrative-theological self-understanding within the wider Vaiṣṇava-Vedāntic comparative landscape.

engaged_works.title: Sanskrit Vyakhyana on Bhagavata-Purana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Sanskrit commentary in the Mādhva exegetical tradition.
engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit *vyākhyāna* on the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* in the Mādhva exegetical tradition (Bannañje, *Bhāgavata-Vyākhyāna*, Pūrṇaprajña Vidyāpīṭha edition; cf. Introduction). The work continues Madhva's *Bhāgavata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* and supplies the modern Dvaita reading of the *Bhāgavata*'s contested loci — the *catuḥ-ślokī* of book 2 (*Bhāg.* 2.9.32–35), the *Uddhava-Gītā* of book 11 (*Bhāg.* 11.6–29), the cosmogonic passages of book 3 (*Bhāg.* 3.5–7, 3.26–29), and the devotional theology of book 10 (*Bhāg.* 10.29–33 *rāsa-pañcādhyāyī*). The Mādhva contention that the *Bhāgavata* is read decisively on the school's *svatantra*-*paratantra* ontology (Madhva, *Tattva-Saṅkhyāna* 1–4), with Viṣṇu as the *svatantra-tattva* and all other reality as *paratantra*, is sustained throughout (Bannañje *Vyākhyāna* on *Bhāg.* 1.1.1, *janmādy asya yataḥ*, with cross-reference to Madhva's *Bhāgavata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* on the same verse). The commentary is one of the principal documentary instruments through which the Mādhva *Bhāgavata*-tradition contests the Gauḍīya (Jīva Gosvāmī, *Krama-Sandarbha*) and Śuddhādvaita (Vallabha, *Subodhinī*) readings.

engaged_works.title: Edition and Commentary on Madhva's Upanishad-Bhashyas
engaged_works.summary: Critical editions of and Sanskrit commentaries on Madhva's Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas — the Īśa, Kaṭha, Kena, Praśna, Muṇḍaka, Māṇḍūkya, Aitareya, Taittirīya, Chāndogya, Bṛhadāraṇyaka, Talavakāra, and Ṣaṭpraśna commentaries that constitute the school's Upaniṣadic exegetical front (Bannañje, ed., Madhva Upaniṣad-Bhāṣyas, in *Sarva-Mūla-Granthāḥ* vols. 6–10). The work is the modern Mādhva reading of the same Upaniṣadic loci on which the Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita schools have built their rival readings, and supplies the textual base for any twentieth- or twenty-first-century comparative engagement with Madhva on Upaniṣadic interpretation. The treatment of the *mahā-vākyas* — *tat tvam asi* (Chānd. 6.8.7), *aham brahmāsmi* (Bṛh. 1.4.10), *prajñānam brahma* (Aitareya 3.1.3) — fixes the school's reading against the Advaita *jahad-ajahal-lakṣaṇā* (Śaṅkara on Chānd. 6.8.7) and the Viśiṣṭādvaita *ghaṭaka-śruti* (Rāmānuja, *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §§ 26–29) alternatives, and supplies the modern philological warrant for Madhva's distinctive *viṣṇu-paratva* exegesis at each contested *vākya* (Bannañje, *Chāndogyopaniṣad-Bhāṣya-Vyākhyāna* on 6.8.7, with cross-reference to Madhva, *Chāndogya-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya* ad loc.). The work is the school's modern Upaniṣadic reference apparatus.

engaged_works.title: Acharya Madhva: Baduku-Bareha

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus; Sāhitya Akademi recognition.

engaged_works.summary: A Kannada biographical-critical study of Madhva's life and works (Bannañje, *Ācārya Madhva: Baḍuku-Bareha*, Sāgar Publications edition, Udupi), treating the *Sumadhva-vijaya* hagiographical tradition (Nārāyaṇa Paṇḍitācārya, *Sumadhva-Vijaya* sargas 1–16) with critical sympathy and integrating the modern philological evidence on chronology, manuscript transmission, and the historical context of Madhva's polemical engagements (cf. *Baḍuku-Bareha*, chs. on chronology and manuscript history). The work is the standard modern Mādhva biography in Kannada and supplies the school's principal vernacular interface between traditional sectarian narrative and modern Indological method. The treatment of Madhva's polemical engagements with Advaita is constructed to give the school's own self-understanding of the polemical context (*Baḍuku-Bareha*, chs. on the *Daśa-Prakaraṇa* polemical encounters) without sliding into either pure hagiography or pure secular-critical reduction. The book is part of the modern Sāhitya Akademi-recognised Kannada Vedāntic literature and reaches a wider readership than Bannañje's Sanskrit *vyākhyāna* corpus.

engaged_works.title: Stuti-Chandrika on Vayu-Stuti
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Sanskrit commentary on Trivikrama Paṇḍita's Vāyu-Stuti.
engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit *ṭīkā* on Trivikrama Paṇḍita's *Vāyu-Stuti*, the school's canonical praise-poem on Madhva as the third *avatāra* of Vāyu (after Hanumān and Bhīma) (Bannañje, *Stuti-Candrikā*, comm. on *Vāyu-Stuti* verses 1–41; cf. introductory verse on the three *avatāra*-progression). The commentary fixes the standard modern Mādhva reading of the Vāyu-Madhva identification, which is doctrinally consequential for the school's theology of mediating consciousness: Vāyu as the *jīvottama* through whom the supreme Viṣṇu's grace reaches the *jīva*-world (Bannañje, *Stuti-Candrikā* on *Vāyu-Stuti* vv. 22–28, with cross-reference to Madhva, *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* 2.94 on Vāyu's *jīvottama* status). The work integrates Bannañje's critical-editorial sensibility with the school's living devotional register, and shows the continuity between his philological labour on the *Sarva-Mūla* and his pastoral engagement with the Udupi devotional tradition.
